origin: Newfoundland, Canada, developed further in England during the 19th century as a sporting and retrieving dog.
temperament: Friendly, outgoing, high-energy, and eager to please. Excellent with families and children, and highly trainable.
coat_care: Short double coat that sheds heavily, especially seasonally. Weekly brushing, more during shedding season; occasional bathing.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 29-36 kg (65-80 lb); Females: 25-32 kg (55-70 lb).
key_strengths: Versatile working ability, strong retrieving instinct, gentle temperament, good with children and other pets.
potential_health_risks: Hip and elbow dysplasia, obesity, exercise-induced collapse, ear infections, progressive retinal atrophy.

origin: Germany, late 19th century, originally bred for herding and guarding sheep.
temperament: Confident, intelligent, loyal, and protective. Requires firm training and ample mental stimulation.
coat_care: Medium-length double coat with heavy shedding; brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during seasonal 'blowout'.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 30-40 kg (66-88 lb); Females: 22-32 kg (49-71 lb).
key_strengths: Trainability, versatility for police/service work, strong protective instinct, high intelligence.
potential_health_risks: Hip and elbow dysplasia, degenerative myelopathy, bloat (GDV), exocrine pancreatic insufficiency.

origin: Scotland, mid-19th century, bred as a gundog for retrieving waterfowl.
temperament: Gentle, friendly, tolerant, and highly sociable. Excellent family and therapy dog.
coat_care: Long, water-repellent double coat requiring brushing 2-3 times weekly and regular ear cleaning.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 29-34 kg (65-75 lb); Females: 25-29 kg (55-65 lb).
key_strengths: Trainability, patience with children, strong retrieving drive, adaptability to therapy/service roles.
potential_health_risks: Cancer (notably hemangiosarcoma and lymphoma), hip/elbow dysplasia, subvalvular aortic stenosis, hypothyroidism.

origin: France, 19th century, developed from English Bulldogs brought over by lace workers.
temperament: Affectionate, playful, adaptable, and relatively low-energy. Good for apartment living.
coat_care: Short, smooth, single coat with minimal shedding; weekly wipe-down and attention to facial skin folds.
ideal_weight_guide: 8-13 kg (17-28 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Compact size, low exercise needs, sociable and affectionate nature, minimal grooming.
potential_health_risks: Brachycephalic airway syndrome, spinal disorders (hemivertebrae), skin fold dermatitis, heat intolerance.

origin: Germany (with strong development in France), originally a water retriever.
temperament: Highly intelligent, active, eager to please, and elegant in temperament.
coat_care: Dense, curly, hypoallergenic-leaning coat requiring professional grooming every 4-6 weeks and daily brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: 20-32 kg (45-70 lb), varies with height.
key_strengths: Trainability, hypoallergenic coat, versatility in sports and service work, longevity.
potential_health_risks: Addison's disease, gastric dilatation-volvulus (bloat), hip dysplasia, progressive retinal atrophy.

origin: England, developed for scent-based hare hunting, with roots tracing to ancient Greece.
temperament: Curious, friendly, merry, and driven by scent. Can be stubborn during training.
coat_care: Short, dense coat with moderate shedding; weekly brushing sufficient.
ideal_weight_guide: 9-11 kg (20-25 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Exceptional scent tracking, sociable with people and dogs, compact size, low grooming needs.
potential_health_risks: Obesity, hypothyroidism, ear infections (due to long ears), intervertebral disc disease, epilepsy.

origin: Northeastern Siberia, bred by the Chukchi people as a sled dog.
temperament: Energetic, independent, friendly, and prone to wanderlust. Needs strong containment and exercise.
coat_care: Thick double coat, heavy seasonal shedding; brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 20-27 kg (45-60 lb); Females: 16-23 kg (35-50 lb).
key_strengths: Endurance, cold tolerance, pack sociability, efficient energy use for sledding/working roles.
potential_health_risks: Hip dysplasia, progressive retinal atrophy, cataracts, hypothyroidism, zinc-responsive dermatosis.

origin: Mexico, named after the state of Chihuahua, with ancient Techichi dog ancestry.
temperament: Alert, loyal, sometimes feisty, and often bonds intensely with one person.
coat_care: Smooth or long coat variants; smooth needs weekly brushing, long coat 2-3 times weekly.
ideal_weight_guide: 1.5-3 kg (3-6 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Small size ideal for apartments, long lifespan, strong watchdog alertness, low food/exercise needs.
potential_health_risks: Patellar luxation, dental crowding, hydrocephalus, tracheal collapse, hypoglycemia in toy sizes.

origin: Germany, 17th century, bred to hunt badgers and other burrowing animals.
temperament: Bold, curious, stubborn, and playful, with a strong prey drive.
coat_care: Smooth, wire, or longhaired variants; smooth needs minimal care, wire/long need weekly brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: Standard: 7-15 kg (16-32 lb); Miniature: under 5 kg (11 lb).
key_strengths: Tenacity, strong burrowing/hunting instinct, adaptability to apartment life, distinctive loyalty.
potential_health_risks: Intervertebral disc disease due to long spine, obesity, patellar luxation, progressive retinal atrophy.

origin: Border region between Scotland and England, bred for herding sheep.
temperament: Extremely intelligent, energetic, focused, and responsive; needs constant mental engagement.
coat_care: Medium double coat, moderate to heavy shedding; brush 2-3 times weekly.
ideal_weight_guide: 14-20 kg (30-45 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Top-tier trainability, herding instinct, agility performance, problem-solving ability.
potential_health_risks: Collie eye anomaly, hip dysplasia, epilepsy, exercise-induced neurological episodes.

origin: Germany, descended from Roman drover dogs, used historically to herd and pull carts.
temperament: Confident, calm, protective, and loyal, needing early socialization and structured training.
coat_care: Short, dense double coat with moderate shedding; weekly brushing.
key_strengths: Guarding ability, strength and stamina, trainability for working roles, deep loyalty to family.
potential_health_risks: Hip and elbow dysplasia, osteosarcoma, subaortic stenosis, bloat (GDV).

origin: Tibet/China, bred as a companion dog for royalty.
temperament: Affectionate, outgoing, and friendly; well suited to companionship over high activity.
coat_care: Long, flowing double coat requiring daily brushing or a shorter 'puppy cut' with monthly trims.
ideal_weight_guide: 4-7 kg (9-16 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Compact companion size, minimal exercise requirement, affectionate temperament, adaptability indoors.
potential_health_risks: Brachycephalic airway issues, eye ulcers, dental crowding, hip dysplasia, portosystemic shunt.

origin: Persia (modern-day Iran), one of the oldest known cat breeds.
temperament: Calm, gentle, and affectionate; prefers quiet, predictable environments.
coat_care: Long, dense coat requiring daily brushing to prevent matting; regular face cleaning around eyes.
ideal_weight_guide: 3.5-5.5 kg (8-12 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Calm indoor companionship, low activity needs, tolerant of handling, striking appearance.
potential_health_risks: Polycystic kidney disease, brachycephalic breathing issues, dental malocclusion, tear duct overflow.

origin: United States (Maine), one of the oldest natural breeds in North America.
temperament: Sociable, playful, gentle giant personality; good with children and dogs.
coat_care: Long, shaggy, water-resistant coat; brush 2-3 times weekly to prevent tangles.
key_strengths: Large size with dog-like sociability, cold tolerance, good hunting instinct, adaptable temperament.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, hip dysplasia, spinal muscular atrophy, obesity due to large frame.

origin: Thailand (formerly Siam), one of the oldest recognized Asian cat breeds.
temperament: Vocal, intelligent, social, and demanding of attention; forms strong bonds with owners.
coat_care: Short, fine coat with minimal shedding; weekly brushing sufficient.
ideal_weight_guide: 2.5-5 kg (6-11 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: High intelligence, strong human bonding, low grooming needs, playful energy.
potential_health_risks: Progressive retinal atrophy, amyloidosis, respiratory issues, congenital heart defects, crossed eyes.

origin: United Kingdom, descended from Roman-era domestic cats brought to Britain.
temperament: Easygoing, independent, affectionate but not overly clingy; tolerates alone time well.
coat_care: Dense, plush short coat; weekly brushing, more during seasonal shedding.
ideal_weight_guide: 4-8 kg (9-17 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Low-maintenance temperament, tolerance for solitude, sturdy build, calm around children.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, polycystic kidney disease, obesity, hemophilia B (rare).

origin: United States, developed by crossing domestic cats with Asian leopard cats.
temperament: Highly active, curious, athletic, and vocal; needs significant enrichment.
coat_care: Short, dense pelt-like coat with minimal shedding; weekly brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: 3.5-7 kg (8-15 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: High energy and agility, distinctive wild-patterned coat, strong hunting instinct, playful engagement.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, progressive retinal atrophy, patellar luxation, Bengal-specific PK deficiency.

origin: United States (California), developed in the 1960s from domestic longhair cats.
temperament: Docile, affectionate, and relaxed; known for going limp when held ('ragdoll' effect).
coat_care: Semi-long silky coat with minimal matting tendency; brush 2 times weekly.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 6.8-9 kg (15-20 lb); Females: 4.5-6.8 kg (10-15 lb).
key_strengths: Gentle handling tolerance, strong family bonding, calm demeanor, good with children.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, bladder stones, obesity due to placid lifestyle.

origin: Canada, developed in the 1960s from a natural hairless mutation.
temperament: Energetic, extroverted, attention-seeking, and highly social.
coat_care: Hairless or fine down coat; requires weekly bathing to remove skin oil buildup, ear cleaning, and sun protection.
ideal_weight_guide: 3.5-5.5 kg (8-12 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Hypoallergenic-leaning skin (less hair dander spread), strong human bonding, playful and social nature.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, skin sensitivities/sunburn, temperature sensitivity, periodontal disease.

origin: Believed to originate near the Indian Ocean coast/Southeast Asia, popularized via Ethiopia (Abyssinia).
temperament: Active, curious, intelligent, and highly playful; dislikes prolonged confinement.
coat_care: Short, ticked coat with minimal shedding; weekly brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: 3-5 kg (7-11 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: High activity and agility, strong curiosity-driven engagement, low grooming needs.
potential_health_risks: Progressive retinal atrophy, renal amyloidosis, pyruvate kinase deficiency, gingivitis.

origin: Scotland, developed from a spontaneous ear-cartilage mutation in a farm cat in the 1960s.
temperament: Sweet-natured, adaptable, quiet-voiced, and affectionate.
coat_care: Short or long coat variants; weekly brushing for short coat, more frequent for longhair.
ideal_weight_guide: 2.7-6 kg (6-13 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Calm and adaptable temperament, distinctive folded-ear appearance, good with families.
potential_health_risks: Osteochondrodysplasia (cartilage/bone abnormalities linked to the fold gene), arthritis, polycystic kidney disease.

origin: Russia (Archangel region), believed to have origins near the port of Arkhangelsk.
temperament: Reserved with strangers, deeply loyal to familiar people, gentle and quiet.
coat_care: Dense, plush double coat; weekly brushing keeps it in good condition.
ideal_weight_guide: 3-5.5 kg (7-12 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Low-shedding dense coat, quiet demeanor, strong routine-based bonding, hardiness.
potential_health_risks: Obesity if overfed, bladder stones, progressive retinal atrophy (rare).

origin: Equatorial rainforests of West and Central Africa.
temperament: Highly intelligent, sensitive, and capable of advanced vocal mimicry; needs consistent routine.
coat_care: Feather care: daily misting or bathing, regular preening support, and humidity control to prevent feather plucking.
key_strengths: Exceptional cognitive and language ability, strong bonding, long lifespan (40-60 years).
potential_health_risks: Feather plucking from stress, calcium deficiency, psittacosis, aspergillosis (respiratory fungal infection).

origin: Australia, native to arid inland regions.
temperament: Affectionate, social, and easily tamed; enjoys whistling and interaction.
coat_care: Feather care: regular misting/bathing, wing trims as needed, keep dust from crest feathers managed.
key_strengths: Easy to tame and handle, good beginner bird, moderate noise level, strong human bonding.
potential_health_risks: Fatty liver disease, respiratory infections, night frights, feather cysts.

origin: Australia, native to open grasslands and scrublands.
temperament: Curious, playful, and social, especially in pairs or small flocks.
coat_care: Feather care: light daily misting, access to a shallow bath, and regular cage feather cleanup.
key_strengths: Compact size for small spaces, strong flock sociability, capable of learning words, low cost of care.
potential_health_risks: Tumors (relatively common in older budgies), scaly face mites, obesity, psittacosis.

origin: Tropical South America, including the Amazon basin.
temperament: Bold, affectionate, loud, and highly intelligent; needs a large, enriched space.
coat_care: Feather care: daily misting/showering, encourage natural preening, monitor for plucking from boredom.
key_strengths: Strong bonding capacity, impressive lifespan (30-50+ years), high intelligence and trainability.
potential_health_risks: Proventricular dilatation disease (PDD), feather destructive behavior, beak malocclusion, obesity.

origin: Canary Islands, Azores, and Madeira.
temperament: Independent, non-cuddly, valued for song rather than physical interaction.
coat_care: Feather care: shallow bath access several times weekly; minimal handling needed.
key_strengths: Melodic singing (males), low space requirement, low interaction maintenance, good for songbird enthusiasts.
potential_health_risks: Air sac mites, scaly leg mites, egg binding in females, obesity from seed-heavy diets.

origin: Indonesia, native to lowland rainforests.
temperament: Extremely affectionate and demanding of attention; prone to separation anxiety.
coat_care: Feather care: frequent misting/showering to manage dust (powder down), daily preening interaction.
key_strengths: Deep emotional bonding, expressive body language, long lifespan (40-60 years), striking crest display.
potential_health_risks: Feather plucking and self-mutilation from anxiety, psittacine beak and feather disease, obesity.

origin: Sub-Saharan Africa and Madagascar.
temperament: Affectionate, energetic, and can be territorial; forms intense pair bonds.
coat_care: Feather care: daily light misting, shallow bath dish access, regular nail and beak monitoring.
key_strengths: Strong pair bonding behavior, compact size, vibrant coloration, playful energy.
potential_health_risks: Chlamydiosis (psittacosis), feather picking, egg binding, obesity from high-fat seed diets.

origin: Mexico and Central America.
temperament: Bold, confident, vocal, and capable of strong moods; needs experienced handling.
coat_care: Feather care: regular showers/misting, monitor for plucking, encourage natural preening behavior.
key_strengths: Strong talking ability, long lifespan (50-70 years), high intelligence, strong personality bonding.
potential_health_risks: Obesity and fatty liver disease, respiratory infections, feather destructive behavior, vitamin A deficiency.

origin: Netherlands, developed in the 1950s from Netherland Dwarf and French Lop crosses.
temperament: Friendly, calm, and affectionate; adapts well to handling.
coat_care: Short, dense coat requiring weekly brushing, more during molting seasons.
key_strengths: Compact size for indoor housing, gentle disposition, good with children under supervision.
potential_health_risks: Dental malocclusion, ear infections (due to lopped ears), obesity, gastrointestinal stasis.

origin: Netherlands, developed in the early 20th century as one of the smallest rabbit breeds.
temperament: Energetic and can be skittish; needs gentle, patient socialization.
coat_care: Short, flyback coat; weekly brushing, twice weekly during shedding.
key_strengths: Very small footprint, minimal feeding cost, distinct compact appearance.
potential_health_risks: Dental malocclusion (prominent in dwarf breeds), heart issues, gastrointestinal stasis.

origin: Flanders, Belgium, dating back to the 16th century.
temperament: Docile, gentle, and tolerant of handling despite large size.
coat_care: Short, dense coat; weekly brushing, more frequent during heavy shedding due to body size.
key_strengths: Calm giant-breed temperament, good companion for supervised children, striking size.
potential_health_risks: Sore hocks (pododermatitis), obesity, joint/skeletal strain from size, heat sensitivity.

origin: France, early 20th century, notable for a genetic mutation producing plush fur.
temperament: Calm, curious, and generally easygoing.
coat_care: Short, dense, velvet-like coat; gentle weekly brushing to avoid damaging fur texture.
key_strengths: Distinctive plush coat texture, calm handling tolerance, moderate size for housing.
potential_health_risks: Sore hocks due to thin foot fur, dental malocclusion, ear mites, gastrointestinal stasis.

origin: Belgium, developed in the 1990s from a spontaneous mane-gene mutation.
temperament: Playful, curious, and sociable; enjoys interaction.
coat_care: Distinctive mane of long fur around the head; requires daily brushing to prevent matting.
key_strengths: Distinctive mane appearance, compact size, engaging and playful personality.
potential_health_risks: Wool block (GI blockage from ingested fur), matting-related skin issues, dental malocclusion.

origin: Turkey (Ankara region), popularized in England for its wool production.
temperament: Gentle and calm, but requires significant grooming commitment and patience.
coat_care: Very long wool coat requiring daily brushing and regular trimming/shearing to prevent severe matting.
key_strengths: Luxurious wool production, gentle temperament, unique long-coat appearance.
potential_health_risks: Wool block, matting-induced skin infections, heat stress due to dense coat, dental issues.

origin: Andes region of South America, domesticated by indigenous peoples thousands of years ago.
temperament: Social, gentle, and vocal; thrives with companionship of other guinea pigs.
coat_care: Short-haired varieties need weekly brushing; long-haired (e.g., Peruvian) need daily grooming.
key_strengths: Docile and easy to handle, strong social bonding with humans and other guinea pigs, low noise level.
potential_health_risks: Scurvy (vitamin C deficiency), dental malocclusion, respiratory infections, bumblefoot.

origin: Arid regions of Syria and southern Turkey.
temperament: Solitary and territorial with other hamsters; generally docile with individual human handling.
coat_care: Short coat needs minimal brushing; provide a dust bath for self-grooming.
key_strengths: Low space requirement, easy individual care, nocturnal entertainment value, low cost.
potential_health_risks: Wet tail disease, dental overgrowth, respiratory infections, obesity.

origin: Domesticated from the European polecat, likely in the Mediterranean region over 2,000 years ago.
temperament: Playful, curious, energetic, and mischievous; needs supervised free-roam time.
coat_care: Short, dense coat with seasonal shedding; weekly brushing and periodic bathing (not excessive, to preserve skin oils).
ideal_weight_guide: 0.7-2 kg (1.5-4.5 lb), males typically larger than females.
key_strengths: High playfulness and trainability, strong human bonding, entertaining problem-solving behavior.
potential_health_risks: Insulinoma (pancreatic tumor), adrenal gland disease, lymphoma, gastrointestinal foreign body obstruction.

origin: Arid woodlands and deserts of Australia.
temperament: Calm, docile, and generally tolerant of gentle handling; diurnal and interactive.
coat_care: Skin/scale care: requires proper UVB lighting for healthy shedding; occasional lukewarm baths aid shed removal.
ideal_weight_guide: 300-600 g (10.5-21 oz) for adults.
key_strengths: Calm and interactive temperament, manageable size, diurnal schedule matching human activity, expressive behavior.
potential_health_risks: Metabolic bone disease (from inadequate UVB/calcium), impaction, parasites, respiratory infections.

origin: Rocky, arid regions of Afghanistan, Pakistan, Iran, and India.
temperament: Docile, low-maintenance, and generally tolerant of light handling.
coat_care: Skin care: sheds periodically; provide a humid hide to support easy shedding and prevent retained shed.
ideal_weight_guide: 45-90 g (1.6-3.2 oz) for adults.
key_strengths: Low space and maintenance requirements, docile temperament, long lifespan (15-20 years), good beginner reptile.
potential_health_risks: Metabolic bone disease, retained shed (especially toes/tail tip), impaction, tail-related fat storage issues.

origin: Hybrid of African species, popularized as a pet in the United States.
temperament: Solitary, shy initially, but can become tolerant of gentle handling with consistent socialization.
coat_care: Quill (modified hair) care: minimal brushing needed; occasional lukewarm baths to manage skin and quill cleanliness.
ideal_weight_guide: 300-600 g (10.5-21 oz) for adults.
key_strengths: Low space requirement, quiet and low-odor, unique defensive quill behavior, solitary care simplicity.
potential_health_risks: Wobbly hedgehog syndrome (neurological), obesity, mite infestations, dental disease.

origin: Germany, late 19th century, descended from the Bullenbeisser hunting dog.
temperament: Playful, energetic, loyal, and patient with children; retains puppy-like enthusiasm into adulthood.
coat_care: Short, smooth coat with minimal shedding; weekly wipe-down or brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 27-32 kg (60-70 lb); Females: 25-29 kg (55-65 lb).
key_strengths: Strong guarding instinct, high energy for active families, trainability, affectionate nature.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, hip dysplasia, bloat (GDV), brachycephalic breathing issues, cancer predisposition.

origin: Germany, late 19th century, developed by Karl Friedrich Louis Dobermann as a personal protection dog.
temperament: Alert, loyal, intelligent, and fearless; needs firm early training and socialization.
coat_care: Short, smooth single coat; weekly brushing, minimal grooming needs.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 34-45 kg (75-100 lb); Females: 27-41 kg (60-90 lb).
key_strengths: Excellent guard and working dog, high trainability, athleticism, strong loyalty.
potential_health_risks: Dilated cardiomyopathy, von Willebrand's disease, hip dysplasia, wobbler syndrome (cervical vertebral instability).

origin: Germany, developed from mastiff and greyhound-type dogs for boar hunting.
temperament: Gentle, friendly, and affectionate 'gentle giant'; good with families despite imposing size.
coat_care: Short, smooth coat with light shedding; weekly brushing.
key_strengths: Calm giant-breed temperament, good watchdog presence, gentle with children when socialized.
potential_health_risks: Bloat (GDV, high risk due to deep chest), hip dysplasia, dilated cardiomyopathy, bone cancer, shortened lifespan.

origin: England (Yorkshire), 19th century, bred originally to catch rats in textile mills.
temperament: Bold, confident, affectionate, and sometimes feisty with a big-dog attitude.
coat_care: Long, silky, human-hair-like coat requiring daily brushing; often kept in a shorter 'puppy cut'.
ideal_weight_guide: 2-3.2 kg (4-7 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Compact apartment-friendly size, hypoallergenic-leaning coat, strong personality and loyalty.
potential_health_risks: Tracheal collapse, patellar luxation, dental crowding, portosystemic liver shunt, hypoglycemia.

origin: Croatia (Dalmatia region), historically used as a coach/carriage escort dog.
temperament: Energetic, outgoing, and loyal; needs substantial daily exercise and mental stimulation.
coat_care: Short, dense coat that sheds year-round; brush 2-3 times weekly to manage shedding.
ideal_weight_guide: 20-32 kg (45-70 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: High endurance and stamina, distinctive spotted coat, strong bonding with active households.
potential_health_risks: Congenital deafness, urinary/kidney stones (urate stones), hip dysplasia, skin allergies.

origin: United Kingdom, developed as a royal companion breed in the 20th century from older toy spaniels.
temperament: Affectionate, gentle, eager to please, and adaptable to most households.
coat_care: Medium-length silky coat; brush 2-3 times weekly, extra attention to ear feathering.
ideal_weight_guide: 5.4-8.2 kg (12-18 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Excellent companion temperament, adaptability to apartment or family life, gentle with children.
potential_health_risks: Mitral valve disease, syringomyelia, hip dysplasia, ear infections due to long ears.

origin: United States, despite the name, developed by ranchers in the American West for herding.
temperament: Intelligent, energetic, and highly trainable; needs a job or consistent mental engagement.
coat_care: Medium-length double coat with moderate to heavy shedding; brush 2-3 times weekly.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 25-32 kg (55-70 lb); Females: 16-25 kg (35-55 lb).
key_strengths: Outstanding herding and agility ability, trainability, versatility in dog sports, strong work drive.
potential_health_risks: Hip dysplasia, collie eye anomaly, epilepsy, MDR1 drug sensitivity gene mutation.

origin: Swiss Alps, bred as a farm and draft dog by Swiss farmers.
temperament: Calm, affectionate, gentle, and good-natured with families.
coat_care: Long, thick double coat with heavy shedding; brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during seasonal shedding.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 38-50 kg (84-110 lb); Females: 36-45 kg (79-99 lb).
key_strengths: Gentle giant temperament, strong family bonding, cold tolerance, good working/draft ability.
potential_health_risks: Cancer (notably histiocytic sarcoma), hip and elbow dysplasia, bloat, shortened average lifespan (7-9 years).

origin: England/United States, developed as a gundog for flushing woodcock.
temperament: Gentle, affectionate, eager to please, and sociable with families.
coat_care: Medium-length silky coat requiring brushing 3-4 times weekly and regular professional trimming.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 12-16 kg (26-34 lb); Females: 11-14.5 kg (24-32 lb).
key_strengths: Excellent family companion, trainability, good with children, moderate exercise needs.
potential_health_risks: Ear infections due to long ears, progressive retinal atrophy, hip dysplasia, seborrhea (skin condition).

origin: China, one of the oldest dog breeds, bred as a companion for Chinese emperors.
temperament: Charming, affectionate, sociable, and low-energy; thrives on companionship.
coat_care: Short, smooth coat with notable shedding; weekly brushing and regular cleaning of facial wrinkles.
ideal_weight_guide: 6.3-8.2 kg (14-18 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Excellent apartment companion, low exercise requirement, affectionate and easygoing nature.
potential_health_risks: Brachycephalic airway syndrome, obesity, skin fold dermatitis, eye proptosis, hemivertebrae.

origin: Japan, one of the oldest native Japanese dog breeds, originally used for hunting small game.
temperament: Independent, alert, bold, and somewhat cat-like in aloofness; strong-willed.
coat_care: Thick double coat with heavy seasonal shedding; brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 10-13 kg (22-29 lb); Females: 8-11 kg (17-24 lb).
key_strengths: Compact size, low odor, cleanliness (self-grooming habits), strong independent working drive.
potential_health_risks: Hip and patellar luxation, allergies, glaucoma, progressive retinal atrophy.

origin: England, 19th century, developed from bulldog and terrier crosses for sport and companionship.
temperament: Playful, mischievous, strong-willed, and affectionate with an egg-shaped head signature look.
coat_care: Short, flat coat with minimal grooming needs; weekly brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: 22-38 kg (50-85 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Strong build and resilience, playful energetic personality, distinctive appearance and loyalty.
potential_health_risks: Deafness (linked to white coat coloring), heart disease, kidney disease, skin allergies, compulsive tail-chasing.

origin: Newfoundland, Canada, bred as a working dog for fishermen, skilled in water rescue.
temperament: Sweet, patient, and gentle; known as a 'nanny dog' for its calm nature with children.
coat_care: Thick, water-resistant double coat with heavy shedding; brush 2-3 times weekly.
key_strengths: Exceptional swimming/water rescue ability, gentle giant temperament, strong protective instinct without aggression.
potential_health_risks: Hip and elbow dysplasia, subvalvular aortic stenosis, bloat, cystinuria (a hereditary kidney stone condition).

origin: Wales, bred over 1,000 years ago for cattle herding.
temperament: Intelligent, energetic, affectionate, and bold despite small stature.
coat_care: Medium double coat with heavy shedding; brush 2-3 times weekly.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: up to 13.6 kg (30 lb); Females: up to 11.8 kg (26 lb).
key_strengths: Excellent herding instinct in a small package, high trainability, adaptable to family life.
potential_health_risks: Intervertebral disc disease due to long spine, hip dysplasia, obesity, degenerative myelopathy.

origin: Norway, a natural breed adapted to cold Scandinavian climates.
temperament: Independent, gentle, and good climbers; enjoys both solitude and family interaction.
coat_care: Long, thick, water-resistant double coat; brush 2-3 times weekly, more during spring shedding.
ideal_weight_guide: Males: 5.5-9 kg (12-20 lb); Females: 3.6-5.5 kg (8-12 lb).
key_strengths: Cold-weather hardiness, strong climbing agility, calm adaptable temperament.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, glycogen storage disease IV, hip dysplasia.

origin: England (Devon), 1960s, from a natural curly-coat mutation.
temperament: Playful, mischievous, highly social, and dog-like in its attachment to people.
coat_care: Short, wavy, fine coat; minimal brushing needed but regular skin oil management as coat is thin.
ideal_weight_guide: 2.5-4 kg (6-9 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Low shedding, playful and interactive personality, strong human bonding, compact size.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, hereditary myopathy, patellar luxation, skin sensitivity.

origin: Myanmar (Burma), developed further in the United States and United Kingdom in the 20th century.
temperament: Affectionate, sociable, playful, and vocal but softer-voiced than Siamese.
coat_care: Short, glossy, single-layer coat; weekly brushing keeps shine.
ideal_weight_guide: 3.6-5.4 kg (8-12 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Strong human attachment, low grooming needs, playful into adulthood.
potential_health_risks: Diabetes mellitus predisposition, cranial deformities (in some lines), hypokalemia, feline orofacial pain syndrome.

origin: Turkey (Ankara region), one of the oldest natural cat breeds.
temperament: Intelligent, energetic, and affectionate; enjoys interactive play and water.
coat_care: Long, silky, single-layer coat with minimal matting tendency; brush 2 times weekly.
ideal_weight_guide: 2.5-5 kg (5.5-11 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Elegant low-matting coat, high intelligence, playful and social nature.
potential_health_risks: Hereditary deafness (linked to white coat/blue eyes), hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, ataxia (rare).

origin: United States/United Kingdom, developed mid-20th century by crossing Persians with Siamese.
temperament: Calm, gentle, and affectionate, combining Persian placidity with mild Siamese vocal traits.
coat_care: Long, dense coat requiring daily brushing to prevent matting; regular eye area cleaning.
ideal_weight_guide: 3.2-5.5 kg (7-12 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Calm indoor temperament, striking color-point appearance, affectionate lap-cat nature.
potential_health_risks: Polycystic kidney disease, brachycephalic breathing issues, progressive retinal atrophy.

origin: United Kingdom, developed from Siamese breeding lines in a wide array of colors and patterns.
temperament: Highly vocal, social, intelligent, and demanding of interaction.
coat_care: Short, fine coat with minimal shedding; weekly brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: 2.5-5 kg (6-11 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: High intelligence and trainability, strong human bonding, low grooming needs.
potential_health_risks: Progressive retinal atrophy, amyloidosis, dental disease, respiratory sensitivity.

origin: United States, descended from early European domestic cats brought by colonists.
temperament: Easygoing, adaptable, and moderately independent; good all-around family cat.
coat_care: Short, dense coat; weekly brushing, more during seasonal shedding.
ideal_weight_guide: 3.2-6.8 kg (7-15 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Hardy constitution, low-maintenance grooming, adaptable and even temperament.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, obesity, polycystic kidney disease (less common than in Persians).

origin: Isle of Man, a natural breed known for its taillessness from a spontaneous genetic mutation.
temperament: Playful, loyal, and dog-like, often forming a strong bond with one household.
coat_care: Short or long coat variants; weekly brushing, more for longhaired type.
ideal_weight_guide: 3.6-5.5 kg (8-12 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Strong hunting instinct, loyal companionship, distinctive tailless or stub-tailed appearance.
potential_health_risks: Manx syndrome (spinal cord/vertebral abnormalities), arthritis, constipation and bowel issues.

origin: Myanmar/France, associated with legends of sacred temple cats, standardized in France.
temperament: Gentle, affectionate, and calm; sociable without being overly demanding.
coat_care: Semi-long silky coat with low matting tendency; brush 2 times weekly.
ideal_weight_guide: 3.2-5.4 kg (7-12 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Low-matting semi-long coat, gentle even temperament, good with children and other pets.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, congenital hypotrichosis (rare), kidney issues.

origin: Canada, developed by crossing Siamese and Burmese breeds.
temperament: Playful, social, and vocal, blending the energy of Siamese with the affection of Burmese.
coat_care: Short, close-lying coat; weekly brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: 2.5-5.4 kg (6-12 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Highly interactive personality, strong human bonding, moderate low-maintenance coat.
potential_health_risks: Progressive retinal atrophy, dental disease, respiratory sensitivity, amyloidosis (rare).

origin: Egypt, one of the oldest natural spotted cat breeds, with imagery dating to ancient Egypt.
temperament: Athletic, alert, and loyal, often reserved with strangers but affectionate with family.
coat_care: Short, dense, naturally spotted coat; weekly brushing.
ideal_weight_guide: 2.5-5 kg (5.5-11 lb) for both sexes.
key_strengths: Exceptional speed and agility (one of the fastest domestic cats), naturally occurring spotted coat, strong bonding with familiar people.
potential_health_risks: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, patellar luxation, sensitivity to anesthesia.

origin: Northeastern South America (Venezuela, Brazil, Guyana).
temperament: Playful, affectionate, and very loud; needs significant social interaction.
coat_care: Feather care: daily misting/showering to keep vivid plumage healthy, monitor for feather picking.
key_strengths: Vibrant coloration, strong social bonding, playful and acrobatic behavior.
potential_health_risks: Feather picking from stress or boredom, psittacosis, beak malocclusion, vitamin A deficiency.

origin: New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and northeastern Australia.
temperament: Calm, gentle, and less prone to biting than many parrots; females can be more assertive than males.
coat_care: Feather care: frequent misting/showering, high humidity preference; monitor for feather damaging behavior.
key_strengths: Striking sexual dimorphism in color, calm demeanor, strong verbal mimicry ability.
potential_health_risks: Toe-tapping/wing-flipping syndrome, feather picking, sensitivity to dietary imbalance, obesity from high-fat diets.

origin: Temperate and subtropical South America (Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay).
temperament: Intelligent, social, and talkative; can be territorial over its cage/nest area.
coat_care: Feather care: regular misting/bathing, standard preening support.
key_strengths: Strong talking ability, high intelligence, notable social/nest-building behavior, hardy constitution.
potential_health_risks: Fatty liver disease, feather picking, self-mutilation from stress, respiratory infections.

origin: South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, widely distributed native range.
temperament: Intelligent and capable of extensive vocabulary, though can be nippy during adolescence.
coat_care: Feather care: daily misting or regular baths to maintain plumage sheen.
key_strengths: Excellent talking ability, vivid coloration, long lifespan (25-30 years).
potential_health_risks: Psittacine beak and feather disease, obesity, feather plucking, respiratory infections.

origin: Central Australia and parts of Indonesia.
temperament: Social and active, best kept in pairs or small flocks; not typically hand-tame.
coat_care: Feather care: shallow bath access several times weekly; minimal direct handling needed.
key_strengths: Low space requirement, minimal noise, easy breeding and flock behavior, low-cost care.
potential_health_risks: Air sac mites, scaly leg mites, egg binding in females, obesity from seed-heavy diets.

origin: Descended from the wild rock dove, domesticated thousands of years ago across the Mediterranean and Middle East.
temperament: Calm, docile, and highly trainable for homing and show purposes.
coat_care: Feather care: regular access to shallow bath water; preening is largely self-managed.
key_strengths: Strong homing instinct, hardy and low-maintenance, long-standing history of trainability.
potential_health_risks: Canker (trichomoniasis), paramyxovirus, external parasites (lice/mites), respiratory infections.

origin: Western Ecuador and Peru.
temperament: Bold and feisty despite small size; can be territorial but bonds strongly with owners.
coat_care: Feather care: light daily misting, shallow bath dish access.
key_strengths: Compact size for small living spaces, big-parrot personality, strong one-on-one bonding.
potential_health_risks: Feather picking, obesity, egg binding in females, respiratory infections.

origin: Amazon basin of Brazil, Peru, Colombia, and Ecuador.
temperament: Extremely playful, high-energy, and clownish; needs ample physical and mental stimulation.
coat_care: Feather care: frequent misting/showering to support activity level and skin health.
key_strengths: Highly entertaining acrobatic behavior, strong bonding, distinctive playful personality.
potential_health_risks: Feather picking from insufficient stimulation, obesity, proventricular dilatation disease.

origin: Central and eastern South America, primarily Brazil.
temperament: Gentle and affectionate despite its large size; described as a 'gentle giant' among macaws.
coat_care: Feather care: daily misting/showering, requires strong beak conditioning via chewable enrichment.
key_strengths: Largest flying parrot species, gentle temperament for its size, strong human bonding, striking cobalt plumage.
potential_health_risks: Proventricular dilatation disease, vitamin/mineral deficiencies (especially calcium), obesity, beak malocclusion.

origin: South America, including Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina.
temperament: Affectionate, playful, and generally quieter than other conure species.
coat_care: Feather care: daily light misting, shallow bath access.
key_strengths: Quieter vocalization relative to other parrots, compact size, strong one-on-one bonding.
potential_health_risks: Feather picking, psittacosis, beak malocclusion, obesity from seed-heavy diets.

origin: United States, developed in the 1980s from standard Rex rabbits for smaller size.
temperament: Calm, friendly, and easy to handle; popular as a pet and show breed.
coat_care: Short, dense, velvet-like coat; gentle weekly brushing.
key_strengths: Compact plush-coated companion, calm temperament, manageable size for indoor housing.
potential_health_risks: Sore hocks, dental malocclusion, gastrointestinal stasis, ear mites.

origin: Netherlands (despite the name, developed and standardized in England from Dutch imports).
temperament: Friendly, calm, and one of the most even-tempered rabbit breeds.
coat_care: Short, flyback coat; weekly brushing, twice weekly during shedding.
key_strengths: Distinctive banded coat pattern, gentle temperament good for children, moderate size.
potential_health_risks: Dental malocclusion, gastrointestinal stasis, obesity, uterine cancer in unspayed females.

origin: United States (California), developed in the 1920s for meat and fur production.
temperament: Docile and calm, generally easy to handle.
coat_care: Short, dense coat; weekly brushing.
key_strengths: Calm handling tolerance, sturdy build, good for both companion and show purposes.
potential_health_risks: Sore hocks, obesity, dental malocclusion, gastrointestinal stasis.

origin: United States, developed in the early 20th century, historically raised for fur and meat.
temperament: Gentle and calm, tends to be a quiet, easygoing breed.
coat_care: Short, dense coat; weekly brushing.
key_strengths: Docile temperament, hardy constitution, distinctive solid blue or white coat.
potential_health_risks: Sore hocks, gastrointestinal stasis, dental malocclusion, obesity.

origin: Uncertain, likely Asia; one of the oldest recognized rabbit breeds.
temperament: Gentle, quiet, and docile; often considered one of the calmest breeds.
coat_care: Short, fine coat; weekly brushing.
key_strengths: Very docile and easy to handle, distinctive color-point markings, small manageable size.
potential_health_risks: Dental malocclusion, gastrointestinal stasis, sore hocks, heat sensitivity.

origin: France, late 19th century, known for distinctive brindle/patchwork coat coloring.
temperament: Active, curious, and outgoing compared to some calmer breeds.
coat_care: Short, dense coat; weekly brushing.
key_strengths: Striking multicolor pattern, energetic and engaging personality, moderate size.
potential_health_risks: Gastrointestinal stasis, dental malocclusion, sore hocks, obesity.

origin: United States (Ohio), developed in the early 20th century, historically bred for fur.
temperament: Docile, calm, and known for enjoying being held more than many rabbit breeds.
coat_care: Dense, standing coat with a silvered effect; brush weekly, more during molt.
key_strengths: Unusual upright fur texture, calm handleable temperament, good size for companionship.
potential_health_risks: Sore hocks, gastrointestinal stasis, dental malocclusion, obesity due to larger frame.

origin: England, developed from the English Silver rabbit, one of the smallest domestic rabbit breeds.
temperament: Energetic, alert, and can be skittish; described as having a 'racy' active temperament.
coat_care: Short, fine coat; weekly brushing.
key_strengths: Very small size, low feed cost, active energetic personality.
potential_health_risks: Dental malocclusion, gastrointestinal stasis, heart issues, fragility due to small size.

origin: Andes Mountains of South America (Chile, Peru, Bolivia, Argentina).
temperament: Curious, active, and skittish around loud noise; mostly nocturnal.
coat_care: Extremely dense fur requiring regular dust baths (not water) to prevent matting and maintain skin health.
key_strengths: Ultra-soft dense coat, long lifespan (10-20 years), low-odor droppings, engaging nocturnal activity.
potential_health_risks: Dental malocclusion, gastrointestinal stasis, fur ring (penile fur entrapment in males), heatstroke.

origin: Domesticated from the wild brown rat, with organized breeding beginning in 19th century England.
temperament: Intelligent, social, and affectionate; thrives with companionship of other rats.
coat_care: Short coat requiring minimal grooming; rats self-groom frequently.
key_strengths: High intelligence and trainability, strong human bonding, low space and cost requirements.
potential_health_risks: Mammary tumors (especially in unspayed females), respiratory infections (mycoplasma), obesity.

origin: Arid regions of Mongolia and northern China.
temperament: Curious, active, and social; best kept in same-sex pairs or small groups.
coat_care: Short coat requiring minimal care; provide a sand bath for self-grooming.
key_strengths: Low odor due to desert-adapted low water needs, active burrowing behavior, easy low-cost care.
potential_health_risks: Epileptic seizures (stress-triggered in some lines), tail degloving injuries, dental overgrowth, obesity.

origin: Central Chile, native to matorral shrubland and mountainous terrain.
temperament: Highly social, diurnal, and vocal; needs group housing and extensive enrichment.
coat_care: Short, dense coat; requires regular dust baths similar to chinchillas to maintain coat health.
key_strengths: Diurnal schedule matching human activity, strong social intelligence, engaging vocal communication.
potential_health_risks: Diabetes (highly sugar-sensitive), dental malocclusion, cataracts, tail degloving injuries.

origin: Australia, Indonesia, and Papua New Guinea, native to eucalyptus forests.
temperament: Social, nocturnal, and bonds closely with owners; distress-prone if kept solitary.
coat_care: Soft fur requiring minimal grooming; self-grooming is typical, occasional gentle brushing.
key_strengths: Unique gliding ability, strong social bonding (pouch-carrying behavior with owners), long lifespan (10-15 years).
potential_health_risks: Metabolic bone disease from calcium-poor diet, stress-related self-mutilation, obesity, dental disease.

origin: Southeastern United States, particularly the Mississippi River basin.
temperament: Alert and food-motivated; largely solitary but can recognize caretakers.
coat_care: Shell/skin care: requires proper UVB lighting and basking area to prevent shell rot and support healthy shedding of scutes.
ideal_weight_guide: 0.5-1.1 kg (1-2.5 lb) for adults, varies by shell size.
key_strengths: Long lifespan (20-30+ years), engaging aquatic behavior, relatively low daily interaction needs.
potential_health_risks: Shell rot, metabolic bone disease, respiratory infections, vitamin A deficiency.

origin: Central Asia, including regions of Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, and Afghanistan.
temperament: Curious, active for a tortoise, and generally tolerant of gentle handling.
coat_care: Shell care: requires UVB lighting, proper substrate for burrowing, and periodic shell inspection for healthy growth rings.
ideal_weight_guide: 0.4-0.9 kg (14-32 oz) for adults.
key_strengths: Compact size relative to other tortoises, long lifespan (40-50+ years), hardy and active temperament.
potential_health_risks: Metabolic bone disease, respiratory infections, shell pyramiding from improper diet/UVB, parasites.

origin: West and Central Africa, native to grasslands and open forests.
temperament: Docile and shy, often coiling into a ball defensively rather than being aggressive.
coat_care: Skin care: requires proper humidity for clean sheds; provide a humid hide during shedding periods.
ideal_weight_guide: 1.5-2.3 kg (3.3-5 lb) for adults.
key_strengths: Calm, low-maintenance temperament, manageable size, long lifespan (20-30 years), minimal noise/odor.
potential_health_risks: Respiratory infections, retained shed, mites, scale rot from excess humidity/wet substrate.

origin: Southeastern United States, native to fields, woodlands, and abandoned structures.
temperament: Docile, curious, and generally easy to handle, making it a popular beginner snake.
coat_care: Skin care: moderate humidity for clean shedding; provide a humid hide during shed cycles.
ideal_weight_guide: 450-900 g (1-2 lb) for adults.
key_strengths: Easygoing temperament, manageable size, hardy constitution, wide range of color morphs.
potential_health_risks: Respiratory infections, retained shed (especially eye caps and tail tip), mites, mouth rot (infectious stomatitis).

origin: Yemen and Saudi Arabia, native to mountainous, humid regions of the Arabian Peninsula.
temperament: Solitary and territorial; dislikes handling and does best as a 'look but don't touch' pet.
coat_care: Skin care: requires high humidity, misting/dripper systems, and strong UVB lighting to support healthy shedding.
ideal_weight_guide: 100-200 g (3.5-7 oz) for adults.
key_strengths: Striking color-changing display, low interaction/handling requirement, visually engaging enclosure animal.
potential_health_risks: Metabolic bone disease, respiratory infections, dehydration, egg binding in females.

origin: Lake Xochimilco, Mexico, a neotenic salamander that remains aquatic throughout life.
temperament: Solitary, sedentary, and low-interaction; best observed rather than handled.
coat_care: Skin/gill care: requires clean, cool, well-filtered water and gentle handling to avoid damaging its sensitive external gills and mucus layer.
ideal_weight_guide: 60-110 g (2-4 oz) for adults.
key_strengths: Unique regenerative abilities, low-noise aquatic care, visually distinctive external gills, long lifespan (10-15 years).
potential_health_risks: Bacterial and fungal skin infections, ammonia poisoning from poor water quality, impaction from substrate ingestion.

origin: Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam, native to shallow rice paddies and slow-moving waters.
temperament: Solitary and territorial, especially males, which cannot be housed together.
coat_care: Fin/scale care: requires clean, stable, warm water conditions and gentle handling to protect flowing fins from damage or fin rot.
ideal_weight_guide: 2-3 g (0.07-0.1 oz) for adults.
key_strengths: Vivid coloration and finnage, low space requirement, hardy in stable water conditions.
potential_health_risks: Fin rot, ich (parasitic infection), swim bladder disorder, dropsy (bacterial infection).

origin: China, domesticated over 1,000 years ago from wild Prussian carp.
temperament: Social and active among their own kind; benefit from being kept in groups.
coat_care: Scale/slime coat care: requires clean, well-filtered, adequately sized water to protect the fish's protective slime coat.
ideal_weight_guide: Varies widely by variety, typically 100-500 g (3.5-17.6 oz) for adults in adequate space.
key_strengths: Long lifespan (10-20+ years with proper care), hardy in cooler water, wide variety of ornamental forms.
potential_health_risks: Swim bladder disorder, ich, fin rot, ammonia/nitrite poisoning from undersized tanks.

origin: Coastal tropical regions, including the Caribbean and Indo-Pacific.
temperament: Social among their own kind, generally docile, and mostly nocturnal.
coat_care: Exoskeleton care: requires humidity control for molting and access to appropriately sized replacement shells.
ideal_weight_guide: Varies by species and shell size; typically a few grams to under 100 g depending on maturity.
key_strengths: Low daily interaction requirement, long lifespan when well cared for (can exceed 10-20 years), engaging shell-changing behavior.
potential_health_risks: Molting complications from low humidity, shell-related stress, bacterial infections, dehydration.

origin: Chile and Argentina, native to arid scrubland and desert regions.
temperament: Docile and slow-moving for a tarantula, though can be defensive if startled.
coat_care: Exoskeleton/hair care: minimal direct care; provide appropriate humidity and substrate to support molting.
ideal_weight_guide: 15-30 g (0.5-1 oz) for adults.
key_strengths: Low daily maintenance, long lifespan for females (15-20+ years), calm temperament relative to other tarantula species.
potential_health_risks: Dehydration, mold/mite issues in overly humid enclosures, molting complications, stress-induced hair kicking (defensive behavior).

origin: Japan, developed as a companion and/or hunting breed with regional working traditions.
temperament: Alert, loyal, and intelligent, with a strong work ethic. Benefits from early socialization and consistent positive training.
coat_care: Short coat; weekly brushing is usually sufficient, with more frequent grooming during shedding periods.
ideal_weight_guide: Small: about 3-10 kg (7-22 lb), depending on sex and build.
key_strengths: Trainability, adaptability, and suitability for active households; often excels in obedience, sport, or practical work.
potential_health_risks: Hip and elbow dysplasia, obesity, dental disease, and breed-associated eye or cardiac conditions may occur.

origin: United States, developed for companionship, sport, or practical working roles.
temperament: Friendly and adaptable, often affectionate with family. Usually thrives with regular exercise, training, and social interaction.
coat_care: Medium double coat; brush 2-3 times weekly and increase grooming during seasonal shedding.
ideal_weight_guide: Small-to-medium: about 7-15 kg (15-33 lb), depending on sex and build.
key_strengths: Strong companionship qualities, loyalty, and versatility across family, sporting, or working roles.
potential_health_risks: Breed-associated orthopedic problems, allergies, ear disease, and obesity can be concerns; routine veterinary screening is important.

origin: United Kingdom, developed through traditional hunting, herding, or companion-dog breeding.
temperament: Confident and energetic, with a natural instinct for work or activity. Needs mental stimulation and structured daily exercise.
coat_care: Long or feathered coat; frequent brushing, often several times per week, helps prevent mats and tangles.
ideal_weight_guide: Medium: about 15-25 kg (33-55 lb), depending on sex and build.
key_strengths: Endurance, intelligence, and eagerness to engage with people; often responds well to structured activities.
potential_health_risks: Heart disease, eye disorders, joint problems, and gastric dilatation-volvulus may be relevant depending on breed and size.

origin: Germany and Central Europe, developed for working, guarding, or sporting purposes.
temperament: Gentle and people-oriented, often forming close bonds with caregivers. Can be sensitive and responds well to reward-based training.
coat_care: Wire or textured coat; regular brushing and periodic professional grooming or hand-stripping may be needed.
ideal_weight_guide: Medium-to-large: about 20-35 kg (44-77 lb), depending on sex and build.
key_strengths: Distinctive working instincts and strong human bond, with potential for therapy, sport, or specialized roles.
potential_health_risks: Skin disease, orthopedic issues, and hereditary eye conditions can occur; responsible breeding and regular veterinary care help reduce risk.

origin: France and Western Europe, developed through regional hunting, farm, or companion-dog traditions.
temperament: Independent yet devoted, with a distinctive personality. Early socialization and clear boundaries help support good behavior.
coat_care: Curly or wool-like coat; routine brushing and scheduled trimming help maintain coat condition.
ideal_weight_guide: Large: about 30-50 kg (66-110 lb), depending on sex and build.
key_strengths: Compact or adaptable size with a lively personality, making it suitable for many home environments when exercise needs are met.
potential_health_risks: Patellar luxation, dental disease, obesity, and age-related joint problems may occur depending on size and conformation.

origin: Scandinavia, developed for cold climates, hunting, herding, or farm work.
temperament: Alert, loyal, and intelligent, with a strong work ethic. Benefits from early socialization and consistent positive training.
coat_care: Short coat; weekly brushing is usually sufficient, with more frequent grooming during shedding periods.
ideal_weight_guide: Giant: often 45-70+ kg (99-154+ lb), depending on sex and individual build.
key_strengths: Trainability, adaptability, and suitability for active households; often excels in obedience, sport, or practical work.
potential_health_risks: Hip and elbow dysplasia, obesity, dental disease, and breed-associated eye or cardiac conditions may occur.

origin: Spain and Southern Europe, developed for hunting, guarding, or companionship.
temperament: Friendly and adaptable, often affectionate with family. Usually thrives with regular exercise, training, and social interaction.
coat_care: Medium double coat; brush 2-3 times weekly and increase grooming during seasonal shedding.
ideal_weight_guide: Small: about 3-10 kg (7-22 lb), depending on sex and build.
key_strengths: Strong companionship qualities, loyalty, and versatility across family, sporting, or working roles.
potential_health_risks: Breed-associated orthopedic problems, allergies, ear disease, and obesity can be concerns; routine veterinary screening is important.

origin: Central Asia and Eastern Europe, developed for livestock guarding, hunting, or endurance work.
temperament: Confident and energetic, with a natural instinct for work or activity. Needs mental stimulation and structured daily exercise.
coat_care: Long or feathered coat; frequent brushing, often several times per week, helps prevent mats and tangles.
ideal_weight_guide: Small-to-medium: about 7-15 kg (15-33 lb), depending on sex and build.
key_strengths: Endurance, intelligence, and eagerness to engage with people; often responds well to structured activities.
potential_health_risks: Heart disease, eye disorders, joint problems, and gastric dilatation-volvulus may be relevant depending on breed and size.

coat_care: Wire or textured coat; regular brushing and periodic professional grooming or hand-stripping may be needed.

key_strengths: Distinctive working instincts and strong human bond, with potential for therapy, sport, or specialized roles.

origin: United Kingdom, developed through selective breeding for meat, fur, exhibition, or companionship.
temperament: Gentle, curious, and social, often becoming affectionate with patient handling. Benefits from companionship and daily exercise.
coat_care: Short, dense coat; brush weekly and increase grooming during seasonal shedding to reduce swallowed hair.
ideal_weight_guide: Small: about 0.8-1.5 kg (1.8-3.3 lb), depending on variety.
key_strengths: Friendly companionship, quiet household presence, and strong suitability for indoor living with appropriate exercise space.
potential_health_risks: Dental overgrowth, gastrointestinal stasis, obesity, and respiratory disease are important health concerns; high-fiber diets are essential.

origin: France and Western Europe, developed from regional domestic rabbit stocks for varied production and show traits.
temperament: Alert and inquisitive, with a playful personality. Needs safe exercise space, enrichment, and gradual socialization.
coat_care: Medium coat; brush several times per week, especially around the hindquarters and during shedding seasons.
ideal_weight_guide: Small-to-medium: about 1.2-2.5 kg (2.6-5.5 lb), depending on variety.
key_strengths: Distinctive coat or markings, gentle behavior, and good potential for bonding with patient caregivers.
potential_health_risks: Malocclusion, gastrointestinal stasis, sore hocks, and obesity may occur; regular dental and weight monitoring is recommended.

origin: United States, developed through selective breeding for exhibition, fur, meat, or companionship.
temperament: Generally calm and friendly, though individual personalities vary. Consistent handling and a predictable environment encourage trust.
coat_care: Long wool or angora-type coat; requires frequent, sometimes daily, grooming to prevent mats and fiber ingestion.
ideal_weight_guide: Medium: about 2-4 kg (4.4-8.8 lb), depending on variety.
key_strengths: Variety of sizes and coat types, with many individuals enjoying litter training and interactive enrichment.
potential_health_risks: Uterine disease in unspayed females, dental problems, and gastrointestinal disorders are potential concerns.

origin: Belgium and Central Europe, developed from European rabbit breeding traditions and later standardized as show breeds.
temperament: Active and curious, often enjoying tunnels, digging opportunities, and foraging enrichment.
coat_care: Rex-type plush coat; gentle regular brushing is usually sufficient, avoiding overly aggressive grooming.
ideal_weight_guide: Large: about 4-6.5 kg (8.8-14.3 lb), depending on variety.
key_strengths: Quiet nature, social potential, and engaging behaviors such as foraging, digging, and binkying.
potential_health_risks: Flystrike, dental disease, gastrointestinal stasis, and spinal injuries can occur; careful hygiene and safe handling are important.

origin: Germany and neighboring Europe, developed through structured breeding programs emphasizing coat, size, or pattern.
temperament: Sweet-natured and people-oriented when well socialized, with a need for gentle handling and plenty of room to move.
coat_care: Fine or silky coat; routine brushing helps prevent tangles and keeps the coat clean.
ideal_weight_guide: Giant: often 6 kg (13 lb) or more, depending on variety and individual.
key_strengths: Adaptability to well-managed indoor housing and strong companionship potential when diet and dental care are appropriate.
potential_health_risks: Respiratory disease, dental problems, obesity, and reproductive disorders may occur; routine rabbit-savvy veterinary care is valuable.

ideal_weight_guide: Giant: often 6 kg (13 lb) or more, depending on variety and individual.
key_strengths: Adaptability to well-managed indoor housing and strong companionship potential when diet and dental care are appropriate.
potential_health_risks: Respiratory disease, dental problems, obesity, and reproductive disorders may occur; routine rabbit-savvy veterinary care is valuable.

origin: Belgium and Central Europe, developed from European rabbit breeding traditions and later standardized as show breeds.
temperament: Gentle, curious, and social, often becoming affectionate with patient handling. Benefits from companionship and daily exercise.
coat_care: Short, dense coat; brush weekly and increase grooming during seasonal shedding to reduce swallowed hair.
ideal_weight_guide: Small: about 0.8-1.5 kg (1.8-3.3 lb), depending on variety.
key_strengths: Friendly companionship, quiet household presence, and strong suitability for indoor living with appropriate exercise space.
potential_health_risks: Dental overgrowth, gastrointestinal stasis, obesity, and respiratory disease are important health concerns; high-fiber diets are essential.

origin: Germany and neighboring Europe, developed through structured breeding programs emphasizing coat, size, or pattern.
temperament: Alert and inquisitive, with a playful personality. Needs safe exercise space, enrichment, and gradual socialization.
coat_care: Medium coat; brush several times per week, especially around the hindquarters and during shedding seasons.
ideal_weight_guide: Small-to-medium: about 1.2-2.5 kg (2.6-5.5 lb), depending on variety.
key_strengths: Distinctive coat or markings, gentle behavior, and good potential for bonding with patient caregivers.
potential_health_risks: Malocclusion, gastrointestinal stasis, sore hocks, and obesity may occur; regular dental and weight monitoring is recommended.

origin: Netherlands and Northern Europe, developed through local rabbit breeding and exhibition traditions.
temperament: Generally calm and friendly, though individual personalities vary. Consistent handling and a predictable environment encourage trust.
coat_care: Long wool or angora-type coat; requires frequent, sometimes daily, grooming to prevent mats and fiber ingestion.
ideal_weight_guide: Medium: about 2-4 kg (4.4-8.8 lb), depending on variety.
key_strengths: Variety of sizes and coat types, with many individuals enjoying litter training and interactive enrichment.
potential_health_risks: Uterine disease in unspayed females, dental problems, and gastrointestinal disorders are potential concerns.
